headline: On third vote, Idaho budget committee restores funding for military tuition benefits for Guard
summary: The Idaho Legislature's Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee voted 17-1 to restore $190,800 in funding cuts to the Idaho Military Division's State Education Assistance Program, maintaining tuition assistance funding at 69% of its FY2026 level rather than dropping to 39%. The restoration follows two prior rejections of the same proposal in February and allows National Guard members to receive up to $8,000 annually for tuition at Idaho institutions. The program is considered critical for Guard recruitment and retention. The adjusted fiscal year 2027 budget must still be approved by the full Idaho Senate and House. The vote represents a reversal of the committee's earlier position on military education benefits within the state budget.

headline: Idaho budget committee provides one-time funding increase for courts to offset budget cuts
summary: Idaho's Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee (JFAC) imposed 5% general fund budget cuts across most state agencies for fiscal year 2027. To help the Judicial Branch comply, JFAC approved a one-time $3.9 million increase to four dedicated funds, allowing expense shifting from the general fund. This temporary measure prevents immediate cuts to treatment courts, family services, child protection, and technology systems, though officials stress a long-term solution is needed. The mental health court program remains at risk due to separate, unrepaired cuts to Health and Welfare peer-support services. JFAC continues budget deliberations, reflecting sustained fiscal constraint policies that may further impact essential government services.

headline: Idaho state lawmakers delay Medicaid disability budget cut bill
summary: Idaho's state budget faces proposed Medicaid cuts of $21.8 million in state general funds for next fiscal year through House Bill 759, which would reduce provider rates for disability services. Governor Brad Little initiated the $22 million cut proposal as part of budget balancing measures. The Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee has reportedly included these cuts in its base budget planning. House lawmakers temporarily shelved the legislation until March 11 to refine language with stakeholders after testimony from families and providers. The political debate includes tensions over targeting disability services versus Medicaid expansion for able-bodied adults.

headline: NC virtual schools face funding overhaul amid scrutiny of $25M in reserves
summary: The North Carolina State Board of Education is requesting legislative approval to change virtual charter school funding from a county-based system to a statewide per-pupil rate, addressing concerns that the current model bases funding on administrative office location rather than student population distribution; the proposal comes as the state's two largest virtual charters, serving over 6,300 students, hold $25.7 million in reserves while being designated as "continually low-performing," raising questions about funding equity, transparency, and whether virtual schools should receive the same funding as traditional schools given their lower operating costs.

headline: ‘We’re dumping money into the ocean’: Outer Banks seeks state help for long-term NC 12 fix
summary: The article highlights North Carolina's State Transportation Improvement Program (STIP) funding constraints, where $144 billion in projects compete for $31.4 billion, directly impacting state budget allocations for K-12 education; Dare County's $100 million investment in beach nourishment to protect Highway 12 diverts local tax resources that could otherwise support schools, teacher salaries, and educational programs; the $51 million spent by NCDOT on emergency road maintenance over 15 years demonstrates how infrastructure crises consume public funds that compete with education spending in state and county budgets; rural counties' difficulty securing STIP funding due to scoring formulas favoring urban congestion projects mirrors similar challenges rural school districts face in obtaining equitable state education funding; this funding competition framework suggests that permanent infrastructure solutions could free up resources for K-12 priorities.

headline: Avian flu found in three southern Wisconsin counties
summary: The Wisconsin Department of Agriculture, Trade and Consumer Protection is monitoring farm workers for signs of infection following highly pathogenic avian influenza outbreaks in poultry flocks across Dane, Jefferson, and Walworth counties; the agency has established 10-kilometer control areas restricting poultry movement and is culling infected birds to prevent further spread, while recommending biosecurity measures to protect nearby flocks and herds.

headline: Gov. Gordon signs Wyoming’s next two-year budget while vetoing two dozen items
summary: Governor Mark Gordon signed Wyoming's $11 billion two-year budget, vetoing two dozen items over separation-of-powers concerns, with the final plan $53 million below his recommendations after contentious negotiations between the House Freedom Caucus's push for deep cuts and the Senate's support for his proposals. Key budget outcomes include elimination of the SUN Bucks summer food program, conditional $15 million funding for the Wyoming Business Council, and legislative mandates requiring the University of Wyoming to review staffing and restrict diversity programming. Gordon vetoed footnotes directing executive branch employee management and requiring UW to submit a cost-savings plan, asserting unconstitutional legislative overreach into executive functions. The budget process featured a record 247 House amendments, reflecting intense political divisions over spending priorities. The Legislature may override these vetoes with two-thirds majority support in both chambers, though no action had been taken at publication time.

headline: House revives teacher pay raise bill, giving Senate ‘one more bite at the apple’
summary: Mississippi's House and Senate remain gridlocked over K-12 funding after killing each other's teacher pay proposals, with the House now proposing a $5,000 raise (plus $3,000 for special education teachers) while the Senate favors $2,000; the legislative conflict also derailed school choice legislation, exacerbating the state's teacher shortage crisis amid the nation's lowest average salary of $53,704.

headline: Jackson protest focuses on attack on Iran, treatment of Palestinians and crackdown on immigration
summary: On March 5, 2026, Mississippi for a Just World protested at Jackson's Woodrow Wilson and North State Street intersection against the Trump administration's Iran military action, calling it aggressive and unconstitutional while demanding congressional oversight through a war powers resolution after Senate and House Republicans voted down similar measures. Demonstrators, including Iranian-American Delana Karimi-Tavakol, condemned U.S. involvement, treatment of Palestinians, and immigration crackdowns, arguing war spending diverted funds from domestic priorities and disproportionately endangered underprivileged soldiers. Protest leaders urged Mississippians to sign petitions and contact congressional representatives to require legislative approval for continued military operations, framing the issue as an assault on human dignity. The action occurred amid escalating U.S.-Iran conflict following Operation Epic Fury, which killed over 1,230 Iranians including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Organizers connected the protest to the "Woman, Life, Freedom" movement, advocating for resources to support domestic quality-of-life improvements rather than foreign intervention.

headline: Mississippi’s subpar February revenue report comes at bad time as legislators work on budget
summary: Mississippi's February revenue collections fell $65.3 million below estimate (13.3% shortfall), raising concerns that legislators may need to reduce spending forecasts for the upcoming fiscal year budget, potentially jeopardizing a promised $5,000 teacher pay raise; Lt. Gov. Delbert Hosemann holds authority to adjust revenue estimates amid slowing post-COVID collections and economic uncertainties, despite the state maintaining a $2 billion surplus.

headline: See the whole ICEberg: Minnesota students need resources to cope
summary: Operation Metro Surge's immigration enforcement in Minnesota has triggered mass student absenteeism, enrollment declines, and mental health crises; state policymakers must address three measurement gaps—attendance tracking, enrollment rules that cut funding after 15 days of absence, and mental health data—by implementing a statewide attendance dashboard, revising absenteeism definitions, and conducting immediate student mental health surveys to design effective recovery policies.

headline: US Attorney Daniel Rosen defends himself, again, in contempt hearing for ICE order violations
summary: Federal judges in Minnesota are considering contempt charges against the U.S. Attorney after ICE repeatedly failed to return property of immigrants illegally arrested during "Operation Metro Surge." ICE officials claim documents, cash, and tools are lost and further searches would be "futile," while the U.S. Attorney calls the losses "inexcusable" but not intentional. The situation reveals systemic disarray in immigration enforcement during DHS's largest operation, involving 3,000 agents. The controversy has triggered a partial DHS shutdown as Democrats withhold funding to protest ICE abuses, with the government now offering compensation for lost belongings.

headline: Worry for teacher pensions prompts criticism of Oklahoma education funding plan
summary: Oklahoma Senate Republicans propose redirecting $254 million from a Teachers' Retirement System supplement to fund K-12 education initiatives including $2,500 teacher pay raises, additional school funding, and expanded private school tax credits, while limiting the annual pension subsidy to $200 million; the plan draws opposition from teacher unions and retired educators who warn it jeopardizes pension stability and inappropriately uses retirement funds for school choice programs.

headline: Indiana Supreme Court upholds COVID-19 immunity for hospitals in patient death case
summary: The Indiana Supreme Court unanimously ruled that pandemic-era immunity laws shield healthcare providers from liability for complications arising from COVID-19 treatment, dismissing a lawsuit over a patient's death from a bed sore developed while on a ventilator. The decision establishes that courts—not medical-review panels—decide immunity as a threshold legal question, and that protections apply even when death occurs after the official emergency ended, reinforcing broad policy-driven legal protections for medical providers during disease outbreaks.

headline: Morrisey, lawmakers decline to put money in Justice-era emergency food bank fund
summary: West Virginia's FY2027 budget maintains $1.4 million for food banks but eliminates the $10 million Posey Perry Emergency Food Fund, which Gov. Morrisey has excluded from budget requests since 2025. Lawmakers declined to restore the fund despite rising food insecurity affecting one in seven residents and reduced federal food assistance. The decision represents a significant cut from Justice-era funding that previously distributed $6.5 million directly to pantries across all 55 counties, impacting 1.3 million people. While Democrats prioritized adding $25 million for flood prevention, the GOP supermajority controls budget priorities and did not include the food fund. Food bank leaders warn this funding gap will severely limit their ability to meet increasing demand amid ongoing federal cuts and economic pressures.

headline: WV lawmakers rush budget to governor early to deal with any of his potential vetoes
summary: The West Virginia legislature approved a FY2027 budget containing a 5% personal income tax cut costing $125 million in revenue, significantly less than Governor Morrisey's requested 10% reduction, with the $125 million becoming surplus if the cut is not implemented. The compromise allocates $300 million for the Hope Scholarship (a 400% funding increase), $12 million for first-time emergency medical services support, and $5 million for the Flood Resiliency Office Trust Fund. The budget was delivered early to enable legislative override of potential vetoes, reflecting ongoing tensions after the governor issued 29 line-item vetoes last year. Democrats opposed the budget, citing inadequate investment in foster care, public education, and infrastructure while prioritizing tax cuts and voucher programs funded through supplemental mechanisms they deemed fiscally irresponsible.

headline: Rapid responders are mobilizing to fight ICE in red West Virginia
summary: ICE's January 2026 "Operation Country Roads" detained approximately 650 people in West Virginia, prompting federal judges in the Southern District to issue multiple rebukes and release orders; Judge Goodwin described agents as "masked, anonymous" and operating without warrants, Judge Johnston warned the legal theory could permit detaining American citizens, and Judge Berger exposed case sloppiness including mistaken identity, while community attorneys filed successful habeas petitions resulting in dozens of releases and continued providing support services to affected families.

headline: A group seeks to eliminate Ohio property taxes. Experts warn it would create devastating budget cuts
summary: An Ohio ballot initiative to eliminate property taxes—which fund over three-fifths of public schools plus fire, police, and libraries—would require massive state tax increases (sales tax from 5.75% to 15-18% or income tax from 2.75% to 11-15%) or force devastating budget cuts to critical services, while destabilizing public finances due to the higher volatility of replacement revenue during economic downturns.

headline: Financial concerns from Senate result in child care grant and tax credit bills being tabled
summary: The New Hampshire Senate tabled a $15 million child care workforce grant bill (SB 483) due to fiscal concerns amid a growing state budget deficit, after federal officials denied the state's plan to fund it with surplus TANF funds. Senate leadership is delaying all Finance Committee money bills pending assessment of fiscal year conditions later this spring. The move highlights Republican deficit concerns versus Democratic support for child care capacity, while separate legislation advances business tax credits capped at $5 million annually and a scholarship program funded by tobacco and liquor tax allocations.

headline: Gov. Landry, with many ties to Kristi Noem, reacts to her ouster as Homeland Security leader
summary: Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry defended former DHS Secretary Kristi Noem's immigration enforcement record, including aggressive tactics in Minneapolis and Operation Catahoula Crunch, the Trump administration's New Orleans crackdown overseen by ICE Deputy Director Madison Sheahan. Landry's administration receives $1 million monthly from the federal government to house ICE detainees at Louisiana State Penitentiary, following a state emergency declaration to expedite prison renovations. Sheahan, previously appointed by Landry to lead Louisiana Wildlife and Fisheries despite lacking experience, is a former Noem adviser who resigned from ICE to run for Congress.

headline: State House panel weighs restrictions on federal Immigration enforcement in Rhode Island
summary: The Rhode Island House Judiciary Committee heard 15 immigration-related bills, including Rep. Batista's proposal requiring judicial warrants for courthouse arrests after ICE detained a teenage intern, Rep. Felix's legislation banning masked officers, and Rep. Giraldo's bill creating 200-foot enforcement-free zones around polling places. State officials condemned recent ICE courthouse operations for eroding public trust, while the State Police and Police Chiefs Association warned the mask ban could threaten officer safety and create liability. Polls show majority public support for the restrictions. All measures were held for further study.

headline: AG Neronha backs Helena Foulkes. But endorsements whisper. Money talks.
summary: The Rhode Island gubernatorial primary between Gov. Dan McKee and Helena Foulkes—who holds nearly three times the campaign cash—will determine the state's chief executive responsible for proposing budgets, with the election outcome directly shaping fiscal policy decisions on deficits, surpluses, revenue forecasts, and spending priorities.

headline: After bond approval, Woodstock school project faces next tests
summary: Voters in Vermont's Mountain Views School District approved a $112 million bond for a new middle/high school, but the project faces two policy hurdles: district officials must secure 25% of funding from federal, state, and private sources, and the state Legislature must exempt construction costs from the school funding formula's excess spending threshold (118% of state average), which would otherwise double-tax repayment dollars. State Rep. Charlie Kimbell has sponsored legislation to address the formula issue. State school building aid is expected to cover at least 20% of costs. The bond required a 60% supermajority for approval, which was narrowly achieved with 61.1% voter support.

headline: Pritzker part 3: Governor says Illinois ‘prepared’ if feds ramp up immigration crackdown
summary: No article text provided; cannot summarize immigration and enforcement issues.

headline: Michigan AG Nessel files records request seeking details on proposed Romulus ICE detention site
summary: Michigan Attorney General Dana Nessel filed a FOIA request with ICE and DHS seeking details about federal plans to convert a Romulus warehouse into an immigration detention facility, escalating a state-federal dispute over lack of transparency and coordination. The request demands records on property purchase, renovations, impact analyses, and operational timelines. Romulus City Council unanimously opposed the project following community protests, citing concerns about the facility's location near schools and residential areas. State and local officials have criticized federal agencies for failing to provide information about the enforcement facility's scope and development plans. Nessel pledged to publicly release any obtained records, highlighting tensions between federal immigration enforcement priorities and state/local governance.

headline: SC House votes to name roads after Charlie Kirk, Donald Trump, over Democrats’ protests
summary: Partisan legislative efforts to honor Charlie Kirk and Donald Trump through South Carolina road-naming resolutions, strongly opposed by the Legislative Black Caucus over Kirk's racially divisive rhetoric and Trump's felony convictions, may escalate political tensions and generate public demonstrations or civil unrest.
